original_hypothesis.title: Unprotected swap-helper initialization enables malicious swap execution and fund diversion
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: MarginTradingHook.initialize() is publicly callable (no role check). A first caller can set swapHelper to an attacker-controlled contract. Subsequent openPos/increasePos/reducePos operations transfer all tokenIn to swapHelper and rely on it to perform swaps, enabling theft or forced bad swaps.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: swapHelper is a critical trust dependency and is used in coreCallback to receive entire balances. If initialization is not restricted and can be executed by anyone once, a single on-chain transaction can permanently compromise all margin operations.
affected_functions: openPos(uint16 _mode, address _viewer, address _tokenIn, uint _amtIn, address _borrPool, uint _borrAmt, address _collPool, bytes calldata _data, uint _minHealth_e18)
increasePos(uint _posId, address _tokenIn, uint _amtIn, uint _borrAmt, bytes calldata _data, uint _minHealth_e18)
reducePos(uint _posId, uint _collAmt, uint _repayShares, address _tokenOut, uint _minAmtOut, bool _returnNative, bytes calldata _data, uint _minHealth_e18)

inventory_refs.function: openPos(uint16 _mode, address _viewer, address _tokenIn, uint _amtIn, address _borrPool, uint _borrAmt, address _collPool, bytes calldata _data, uint _minHealth_e18)

proof_of_signal.pattern: function initialize(address _swapHelper) external initializer { swapHelper = _swapHelper; } (no onlyGovernor/onlyGuardian); coreCallback transfers ALL tokenIn balance to swapHelper then calls swapHelper.swap(...)

preconditions: MarginTradingHook proxy is deployed but initialize() has not been called yet (or was called with attacker-controlled swapHelper)
Users interact with MarginTradingHook openPos/increasePos/reducePos, triggering coreCallback swaps

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Single unprivileged call to initialize determines the swap execution component for all users.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Deployment-to-initialization window, or any scenario where implementation is upgraded and re-initialization patterns exist (must confirm).

operational_questions: Do deployment scripts call MarginTradingHook.initialize atomically at deployment?
Is swapHelper intended to be immutable/trusted, and is it monitored on-chain?
Can upgrades ever reset swapHelper or re-open initialization paths?
validation_requirements: Review deployment/upgrade scripts for initialization sequencing
Simulate attacker calling initialize first and then stealing tokens in coreCallback swap flow
Check if swapHelper can be changed post-init via any other setter (none visible here)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor mempool for MarginTradingHook proxy deployment
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for users to open/increase/reduce positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: In attackerSwapHelper.swap, transfer received tokens to attacker and/or return manipulated output amounts

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: N/A (not admin); direct component takeover via init
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Margin trading becomes unsafe/unusable; swaps can be reverted or manipulated
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High if no emergency upgrade/role process; requires upgrade to replace hook or swapHelper
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All MarginTradingHook operations that rely on swaps

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: swapHelper address set event absent; must read storage slot or track first initialize tx
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: First tx after deployment calling initialize from non-deployer

validation_result.impact_summary: MarginTradingHook.initialize() has no role check and the constructor omits _disableInitializers(), allowing a front-runner to permanently set swapHelper to a malicious contract that steals all tokens routed through coreCallback swaps.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis identifies a real code defect in MarginTradingHook.sol. The initialize() function at line 63 uses only the `initializer` modifier with no role-based access control. This means the first caller to the proxy can set swapHelper to any address. Critically, unlike every other upgradeable contract in the codebase (InitCore, PosManager, LendingPool, MoneyMarketHook, etc.), MarginTradingHook's constructor does NOT call `_disableInitializers()`. This is a concrete code defect: the implementation contract itself can also be initialized by anyone. Furthermore, swapHelper has no setter function post-initialization—it is permanently fixed after the single initialize() call. In coreCallback, ALL tokenIn balance is transferred to swapHelper (line ~287: `IERC20(swapInfo.tokenIn).safeTransfer(swapHelper, amtIn)`), making it a critical trust dependency. If swapHelper is attacker-controlled, every openPos/increasePos/reducePos operation's swap tokens can be stolen. The vulnerability window exists between proxy deployment and initialization if they are not atomic.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Three distinct code defects compound the risk: (1) Missing _disableInitializers() in MarginTradingHook constructor (deviation from codebase standard), (2) No role check on initialize() beyond initializer modifier, (3) No post-initialization setter for swapHelper. The coreCallback function unconditionally sends all token balance to swapHelper, confirming the impact claim.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Constructor does not call _disableInitializers() (unlike InitCore, PosManager, LendingPool, MoneyMarketHook, etc.). initialize() has no role check. swapHelper has no setter function, making it permanently set by whoever calls initialize first.
All tokenIn balance is unconditionally transferred to swapHelper. A malicious swapHelper can simply keep the tokens and return nothing, or return manipulated amounts.
For comparison: InitCore correctly calls _disableInitializers() in its constructor, as do PosManager, LendingPool, MoneyMarketHook, Config, InitOracle, etc. MarginTradingHook is the exception.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker monitors mempool for MarginTradingHook proxy deployment
2. If initialization is not atomic, attacker calls initialize(attackerSwapHelper) first
3. swapHelper is permanently set to attacker's contract (no setter exists)
4. User calls openPos() -> _increasePosInternal() -> multicall including callback
6. coreCallback transfers ALL tokenIn balance to attacker's swapHelper
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: openPos() -> _increasePosInternal() builds multicall with callback -> IMulticall(CORE).multicall(multicallData) -> InitCore.callback(address(this), 0, abi.encode(swapInfo)) -> MarginTradingHook.coreCallback() -> IERC20(swapInfo.tokenIn).safeTransfer(swapHelper, amtIn) -> IBaseSwapHelper(swapHelper).swap(swapInfo). The swapHelper address was set during initialize() with no access control.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Proxy deploys with atomic initialization via constructor _data parameter
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The TransparentUpgradeableProxyReceiveETH constructor accepts _data parameter that CAN be used for atomic initialization, but we cannot verify it is used.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is feasible if initialization is not done atomically: (1) Proxy is deployed without initialization calldata, (2) Attacker front-runs the deployer's separate initialize() transaction, (3) swapHelper is permanently set to attacker's contract, (4) All subsequent margin operations route tokens through the malicious swapHelper. The missing _disableInitializers() in the constructor also means the implementation contract can be initialized by anyone (though this doesn't directly affect proxy storage). The attack is particularly severe because swapHelper has NO setter function—the only remediation would be to upgrade the proxy's implementation.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL if exploited. All margin trading operations (openPos, increasePos, reducePos) route swap tokens through swapHelper via coreCallback. A malicious swapHelper receives all tokenIn balances and can steal them. Since swapHelper cannot be changed post-initialization, recovery requires a proxy upgrade. The missing _disableInitializers() is a standalone code defect even if the proxy initialization is atomic.
recommended_mitigations: Add _disableInitializers() to MarginTradingHook constructor to match the pattern used by all other contracts
Ensure proxy deployment scripts pass initialization calldata atomically in the constructor
Consider adding a role-gated setSwapHelper() function for emergency recovery
Consider adding an onlyGovernor or onlyGuardian check to initialize() in addition to the initializer modifier
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy MarginTradingHook implementation. 2. Deploy TransparentUpgradeableProxyReceiveETH pointing to implementation WITHOUT initialization data. 3. From attacker EOA, call initialize(attackerSwapHelper) on the proxy. 4. Verify swapHelper is set to attacker's address. 5. Have a user call openPos with tokens. 6. Verify tokens are sent to attackerSwapHelper in coreCallback.
uncertainties: Deployment scripts are not available to verify if initialization is done atomically
The TransparentUpgradeableProxy pattern supports atomic initialization which would prevent the proxy-level attack
The missing _disableInitializers() is a concrete code defect but mainly affects the implementation contract
All other initialize() functions in the codebase have the same lack of role checks, suggesting this is an intentional pattern relying on atomic deployment
additional_testing_needed: Review actual deployment scripts/transactions to verify atomic initialization
Verify on-chain that swapHelper is set to the expected MoeSwapHelper address
Test that the implementation contract's initialize can be called directly (due to missing _disableInitializers())
Confirm no governance mechanism exists to replace swapHelper post-deployment
